nativeRange.habitat: The Redtail Goodeid inhabits shallow rivers, streams, ponds, and springs across a range of conditions, from clear upland tributaries to turbid lowland waterways. Substrates vary from silt and sand to stone, and the species occurs in areas of both standing water and moderate current. The core range lies within the Río Grande de Santiago basin and its tributaries in Nayarit and Jalisco.

decorPreferences: dense planting for cover and fry refuge

diet.notes: Accepts a wide range of foods. A diet with a meaningful plant-based component is recommended — spirulina flakes, algae wafers, blanched spinach or kale, and vegetable-based pellets help prevent the gut issues associated with excessive animal-protein feeding. Supplement with high-quality flake, small pellets, and frozen foods such as daphnia or brine shrimp. Goodeids grazing on biofilm and algae in the tank is normal and beneficial.

goodWith: other goodeid species of similar size (species-only setups preferred)
robust short-finned livebearers in large tanks
avoidWith: long-finned or slow-moving fish (fin nipping risk)
corydoras and small bottom-dwelling catfish (documented aggression including eye damage)

imageCandidates.notes: Male specimen; standard FishBase image. Confirm availability and attribution before use.

summary: The Redtail Goodeid (Xenotoca eiseni) is a hardy Mexican livebearer known for the vivid orange-to-red flush at the base of the tail in males. Listed as Endangered, it is confined to the Río Grande de Santiago basin and tends to need a species-focused setup due to its unpredictable aggression toward tankmates.
careNotes:
The Redtail Goodeid is a goodeid livebearer from highland Mexico and one of the more adaptable members of its family. In 2016, what had long been sold as a single species was split into three distinct species; the name Xenotoca eiseni now applies only to populations from the Río Grande de Santiago basin in Nayarit and Jalisco, while a closely related species, X. doadrioi, occupies a separate endorheic drainage near Etzatlán in Jalisco. Hobbyist stock labelled X. eiseni purchased before the revision may represent either species or a mix of both, and provenance tracking is recommended for conservation-minded breeding programs.

Unlike many goodeids, this species tolerates a broad temperature range — 15 to 30 °C — and generally does not require a heater in typical room-temperature conditions in temperate climates. Cooler water in the 18 to 24 °C range tends to be preferred for long-term health and breeding activity.

Behaviour can be highly variable between individual fish. Some specimens settle well into community aquariums while others relentlessly nip at fins or harass tankmates. The species has a well-documented tendency toward aggression against corydoras and similar bottom-dwelling catfish, with recorded incidents of fin-stripping and eye damage. A species-only setup — or a tank restricted to robust, fast-moving fish of similar size — is strongly advised. Keeping approximately one male to every two or three females helps distribute male attention rather than directing it at a single female.

A meaningful plant-based component in the diet is important; an excess of animal-based protein has been associated with bloating. Supplement dry flake and pellet foods with spirulina, algae wafers, and fresh or blanched leafy greens.
breedingNotes: The Redtail Goodeid reproduces through true internal gestation — a defining trait of the Goodeidae family — in which developing embryos are nourished by ribbon-like internal structures called trophotaeniae that function similarly to a mammalian placenta. This is distinct from the sperm-storage method used by more commonly kept livebearers such as guppies and mollies. Gestation lasts approximately 60 days, after which the female produces 5 to 50 large, fully formed fry measuring around 15 mm. Because females cannot store sperm, each brood requires a separate mating. Fry are immediately self-sufficient and large enough to accept finely crushed flake food from birth. Dense planting greatly improves fry survival, as adults will sometimes consume smaller young. Breeding tends to be straightforward when a stable, well-fed group is maintained in appropriate conditions.

primary.name: SeriouslyFish — Xenotoca eiseni
primary.notes: Primary source. Provided adult size (males 6 cm, females 7 cm), temperature 15–30°C, pH 6.0–8.0, hardness 10–20 dH, minimum tank 55 L / 60×30×30 cm, temperament notes, breeding gestation 7–8 weeks, fry count 5–50, and trophotaeniae biology. Also confirmed species-tank recommendation and aggression toward corydoras.

additional.name: FishBase — Xenotoca eiseni
additional.notes: Cross-checked adult size (males 6 cm, females 7 cm TL), temperature 15–32°C, pH 6.0–8.0, hardness 9–19 dH, taxonomy (Goodeidae, Cyprinodontiformes), IUCN Endangered status (EN, assessed 2018), and native range (Mexico, Central America). Confirmed females can produce young every ~60 days.

additional.name: Fishkeeper.co.uk — Red-tailed Goodeid
additional.notes: Supplementary trade care source. Confirmed temperature 15–30°C, pH 6.5–8.0, dH up to 20, adult size 7 cm, diet (flake, granules, pellets, algae wafers, frozen foods, leafy greens), temperament as “confident, nippy,” fry size ~15 mm, gestation ~60 days, and species-only recommendation.

additional.name: Goodeid Working Group — Xenotoca eiseni
additional.notes: Conservation-focused source. Provided population locality references (El Sacristán Tepic, Río Compostela in Nayarit), confirmed restricted sensu stricto range following 2016 split, and contextualised IUCN Endangered status. Image captions identified male/female specimens from Nayarit populations.

additional.name: Domínguez-Domínguez, Bernal-Zuñiga & Piller (2016) — Two new species of the genus Xenotoca (via GBIF/NovaTáxa summary)
additional.notes: Taxonomic reference for the 2016 three-way split of X. eiseni sensu lato into X. eiseni (Nayarit / Río Grande de Santiago), X. doadrioi (Etzatlán, Jalisco endorheic drainage), and X. lyonsi (Tamazula / Tuxpan rivers). Published in Zootaxa 4189(1): 81–98.

fish.breedingNotes: Gestation lasts approximately 60 days, producing 5 to 50 large fry measuring around 15 mm. Females do not store sperm, so each brood requires a separate mating. Dense planting is recommended to improve fry survival. Fry are large enough to accept finely crushed flake food from birth.
